system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — melodic-rhythms-vol-4

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book teaches jazz improvisation rhythm as a cumulative, modular system built from the bottom up. The pedagogical arc moves in three broad phases: establishing foundational rhythmic awareness through note-grouping displacement (Chapters 1-8), developing polyrhythmic independence through triplet vocabulary and metric superimposition (Chapters 9-16), and synthesizing everything into expressive, compositional fluency through gear-switching, time zones, and rhythmic grammar (Chapters 17-29). Each phase revisits and extends prior material: the 22 one-bar rhythms from Chapter 1 reappear explicitly in Chapter 17; the displacement logic introduced with eighth notes in Chapters 2-3 recurs through triplets in Chapters 9-13 and through 16th notes in Chapter 15. The method is deliberately incremental: sixteenth notes and triplets are withheld from early exercises to sequence cognitive load, then reintroduced systematically.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book's central organizing system is **displacement**: taking a fixed note grouping (two, three, four, five, six, or seven consecutive eighth notes, or their triplet equivalents) and systematically shifting its starting position across every metrical position in the bar. This single technique generates an expanding library of rhythmic patterns without requiring new melodic material. Closely related is **superimposition** (also called metric superimposition or polymetric layering): playing phrases that imply an odd meter (3/4, 5/4, 7/4, 9/4, 11/4) continuously over a 4/4 foundation, creating cross-rhythmic tension. The book introduces precise self-check landmarks for these cycles: the 5/4-over-4/4 pattern aligns on the sixth bar; the 7/4-over-4/4 cycle completes in eight bars.

Key load-bearing terminology includes: **entry points** (the eight metrical positions where a consecutive-note group can begin); **hemiola** (3/4 rhythmic phrases over 4/4, with the explicit arithmetic that four bars of 3/4 equals three bars of 4/4); **hemiovals** (mirror rhythms generated by offsetting a pattern to beat 2 or beat 3-and, creating polymetric illusion through strategic repetition); **time zones** or **gears** (five distinct rhythmic subdivisions: quarter-note triplet, eighth-note, triplet, sixteenth-note, and sextuplet); **expansion and contraction** (reinterpreting a fixed note group across different beat spans, from one beat through four beats, or compressed into 1 1/2 beats); **additive and subtractive** construction of polyrhythmic ratios; and **pocket** (eighth-note placement relative to a triplet pulse).

## Rules and Prescriptions

The book establishes several explicit technical rules. The **harmony anticipation rule** states that later displacement positions (groupings #6, #7, #8 for a four-note group; entries #5-#7 for five-note groups) automatically anticipate the next chord, by approaching its notes, stating it directly, or functioning as approach notes, while earlier positions sound the chord of the moment. The **coexistence principle** for pocket playing prescribes that eighth notes must not become literal triplets but coexist with the triplet feel, distinguishing authentic groove from mechanical mimicry. The **2/4-to-4/4 repetition rule** fits triplet-based phrases into jazz contexts. The **truncation rule** (Chapter 19) for fitting eight-note phrases into triplet subdivisions requires leaving off either the first or last four notes. Chapter 29 codifies six **rhythmic grammar constraints**: never starting or ending on beat one; varying between eighth-note-dense passages and passages without them; and manipulating chord anticipation (half-beat to three-beat offsets) or late resolution (one to four beats).

## Explicit Method and Methodology

The book's overarching method is a four-stage learning loop applied consistently across all material: (1) **structured position-by-position drilling**: practice every entry point or displacement in order; (2) **randomization**: mix starting positions unpredictably to convert rote knowledge into internalized flexibility; (3) **metric superimposition**: layer the now-internalized pattern over a conflicting time signature; (4) **tune application**: deploy the technique over actual harmonic progressions using designated reference tunes. Several chapters make this loop explicit: Chapter 8 completes it with random starts now enriched by polyrhythmic tools; Chapter 10 names the stages as melodic displacement followed by randomized triplet groupings; Chapters 11-12 anchor to Tune 5 before moving to free improvisation.

A parallel mastery benchmark runs throughout: **composition as evidence of internalization**. Chapter 21 requires students to compose their own scale and intervallic sequences in a time zone before proceeding; Chapter 28 assigns twelve modal sequences for composition over backing tracks. The logic is that the ability to generate new material in a vocabulary proves genuine assimilation rather than pattern recitation.

## Perceptual Goals and the Window in the Mind

Beyond technique, the book pursues an explicit perceptual transformation. Chapter 4 names this directly: systematic practice across entry points develops a window in the mind, the ability to hear larger spans of time and form. Chapter 1 establishes its philosophical foundation: internal mental grouping of notes affects articulation and phrasing even when invisible to the listener, connecting thought process directly to physical execution. The displacement and superimposition work throughout is aimed at training the ear to hear rhythmic coherence across bar lines and to internalize phrase-level structure independently of the notated meter.

Chapter 29 frames the culminating goal in compositional terms: silence and endings are active choices, not absences. Rests frame the listener's attention; knowing when to end a solo, placing periods at the ends of musical sentences and paragraphs, invites the next idea rather than forcing continuation. Chapter 27 reinforces this with the concept of the **eighth-note trap**: the tendency to fill every subdivision, which sacrifices dialogue with the rhythm section. The antidote is quarter-note playing and restraint, using limitation as teacher (restricting to half notes and quarter notes, starting with accents and filling around them) so that rhythmic economy becomes as fluent as rhythmic density. The entire method thus moves toward a single horizon: the practitioner who can move freely among time zones, gears, and polyrhythmic layers not by calculating but by feeling, and who shapes that freedom into musical storytelling.

# Chapter 1 — Notes Per Bar

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 introduces the foundational principle that rhythmic sophistication and effectiveness are distinct goals, with the master's characteristic move toward polyrhythmic thinking through note groupings independent of bar-line structure. The chapter teaches that practitioners should develop awareness of note density and bar position by experimenting with different rhythms per bar, then advance to playing specific note groupings across bar lines to generate new rhythmic ideas. A core pedagogical insight that internal mental grouping of notes affects articulation and musical phrasing even when invisible to the listener connects thought process directly to physical execution. The chapter establishes five-note groupings as the entry point into hearing polyrhythms, with the rule that identical note groupings can occupy flexible durations (1 beat to 4+ beats). The 22 one-bar rhythm exercise provides embodied practice scaffolding deliberately limited to quarter and eighth notes, omitting sixteenth notes and triplets to sequence learning deliberately.

# Chapter 2 — 22 Rhythms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 teaches rhythmic displacement: playing the 22 rhythms from Chapter 1 at different metrical positions relative to the bar line. The master's characteristic move is pedagogical: moving students from bar-line-dependent hearing toward phrase-level rhythmic independence by systematically shifting each rhythm early or late (by whole beats or half-beats). The load-bearing concept is that displacement by half-beat intervals (1/2 beat, 1 1/2 beats, 2 1/2 beats) creates perceptually distinct rhythmic patterns, revealing that rhythm is a relational property dependent on its position within the measure. This chapter introduces the terminology of phrasing over the bar line, the ability to hear rhythmic coherence across metrical boundaries.

# Chapter 3 — Eighth-note rhythms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the eighth-note displacement method: students play four consecutive eighth notes and systematically displace their starting point across eight positions in the bar. The chapter introduces the harmony anticipation rule, where later displacements (groupings #6, #7, #8) sound the next chord by approaching its notes or stating it directly, with beat-5 groupings automatically anticipating harmony on their final eighth note. Load-bearing terms include displacement, anticipation, and superimposition, the last extending the method into metric complexity through 3/4 patterns played across 4/4 bars. The progression moves from isolated one-bar patterns through combination into multi-bar phrases, then to random beat placement. A deliberate notational choice keeps eighth-note downbeats even where quarter notes would suffice, preserving transparency of the underlying logic. Displacement functions as a generative principle producing harmonic motion without explicit chord changes.

# Chapter 4 — Playing five consecutive eighth notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches the systematic practice of playing five consecutive eighth notes beginning on different beat positions using Tunes 1, 2, and 3, with attention to harmonic choices when the pattern crosses bar lines: entries #5, #6, and #7 can anticipate the next bar, function as approach notes, or sound the chord of the moment. The pedagogical progression moves from structured drilling across all eight entry points to mixing them up randomly, developing what the master calls a window in the mind that hears bigger spaces of time and form awareness through polyrhythmic perception. Load-bearing terminology includes entry points (the eight positions where five notes can begin) and the harmonic anticipation principle at bar-line crossings. The chapter's characteristic move embeds technical discipline of position-by-position practice within an explicit goal of perceptual freedom.

# Chapter 5 — Playing six consecutive eighth notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches polyrhythmic superimposition: layering odd meters (3/4, 5/4, 7/4, 9/4) over a 4/4 foundation, including starting patterns on the upbeat to create off-beat phrasing. The load-bearing concept is that practicing these cross-rhythmic devices trains the ear to internalize larger structural form, opening awareness of how musicians navigate complex time relationships. The characteristic move is systematic introduction of increasingly complex odd-meter overlays that expand polyrhythmic vocabulary. The chapter is purely instructional technique work aimed at developing form awareness through rhythmic displacement.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

The characteristic move integrates cross-rhythmic devices training the player to internalize rhythmic independence. # Chapter 8 — Playing two consecutive eighth notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 teaches systematic mastery of two consecutive eighth notes through progressive rhythmic displacement and polyrhythmic superimposition. Core drills establish a foundational grid for precise rhythmic placement across four starting beats and their and subdivisions. The pedagogical arc escalates through random beat starting practice, which synthesizes rote drills into internalized rhythmic flexibility, followed by increasingly complex metric superimpositions: 7/4, 6/4, and 11/4 patterns played over a 4/4 foundation. Load-bearing terminology includes polyrhythm, metric superimposition, and time-signature devices. The final synthesis step circles back to random starts now enriched by these polyrhythmic tools, completing a loop from mechanical precision to expressive rhythmic independence.

# Chapter 9 — Triplet groupings

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 teaches triplet groupings as a foundational rhythmic concept, beginning with practicing three consecutive eighth-note triplets starting at different metrical positions within a bar. The chapter introduces superimposing odd-meter groupings over a steady 4/4 pulse, allowing players to develop polyrhythmic independence and displacement awareness. A key technical rule across exercises 10-13 is the selective silencing of triplet subdivisions, using only four of six available notes from two consecutive eighth-note triplets to develop rhythmic precision and selective articulation. Triplet groupings and superimposition serve as load-bearing rhythmic vocabulary for more advanced phrasing work.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 teaches internalization of consecutive eighth-note triplets through two progressive exercises: melodic displacement, where a four-note melody is played starting on different beats within triplet groupings to build rhythmic fluency; and randomized triplet groupings, which escalates practice from structured patterns to unpredictable application. The instructional approach moves from controlled position-based practice to random variation, emphasizing transcending mechanical counting and developing intuitive feel for triplet subdivisions. Key terminology includes melodic displacement as a pedagogical device for embedding triplet internalization and randomized groupings as the advanced stage of mastery.

# Chapter 13 — Three consecutive eighth-note triplets

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 covers three consecutive eighth-note triplets and broader rhythmic expansion: converting eighth-note exercises to sixteenth-note patterns by doubling up (creating double-time vocabulary), the 2/4-to-4/4 repetition rule fitting them into jazz contexts, and mastering hemiola, playing 3/4 rhythmic phrases over 4/4 tunes. The characteristic move builds rhythmic awareness through deliberate practice: conscious counting and foot-tapping train the improviser to hear larger phrase structures (four, eight, sixteen bars) and begin lines from varied beats. Load-bearing terms include hemiola, the precise arithmetic that four bars of 3/4 equals three bars of 4/4, and the distinction between conscious practice and subconscious mastery it builds.

# Chapter 16 — Hemiovals

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

Chapter 16 introduces hemiovals, a rhythmic mirroring technique that creates polymetric effects through displacement. Two constructive rules are taught: a 5/4 mirror rhythm is generated by starting the same rhythm on beat 2 and that was started on beat 1, while a 7/4 mirror rhythm comes from displacing to beat 3 and. The characteristic move: systematic offset of a single pattern produces complex metrical relationships. Load-bearing terminology is mirror rhythm and hemioval, denoting patterns that create the illusion of metric modulation through strategic repetition and timing shifts rather than changes to the underlying pulse.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

# Chapter 18 — 7/4 rhythms over 4/4

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch18.md`._

Chapter 18 teaches the coordination between eighth notes and triplet-based feels, the foundation of playing in the pocket with a jazz rhythm section, alongside the 7/4 over 4/4 superimposition. Loaded terms include pocket (eighth-note placement relative to triplet pulse), laying back (delaying note attacks), and lob (another rhythmic articulation tool). The characteristic move is the coexistence principle: eighth notes should not become literal triplets but coexist with the triplet feel, distinguishing authentic pocket playing from mechanical rhythmic mimicry. The chapter builds this feel through alternating practice (triplet rhythms then eighth notes on the same tune) to internalize the groove rather than intellectualize it.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch19.md`._

Chapter 19 teaches 4/4 rhythms over 3/4 by presenting five interchangeable time zones or gears, expanding vocabulary through systematic gear-switching of melodic lines. The characteristic move is the practical truncation rule for fitting eight-note phrases into triplet subdivisions: leaving off either the first or last four notes to maintain phrasing logic. Load-bearing terminology includes time zones as distinct rhythmic subdivisions (quarter-note triplet, eighth-note, triplet, sixteenth-note, sextuplet gears). The chapter establishes a groove-internalization method: repeat lines at metronome 50-60 on the half note until triplets feel natural when phrased with eighth-note articulation. The rhythms here are twice as long as those in Chapter 20, establishing a structural relationship across the method.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch20.md`._

Chapter 20 covers in the pocket as a rhythmic refinement skill, teaching gear-change practice: spontaneous switching between eighth-note and triplet feels through random rather than programmed application for transferable fluency. Load-bearing concepts include adapting melodic lines to varying time zones (generalizing gear-shifting beyond chord exercises) and the eighth-note to triplet conversion technique, allowing any scale or intervallic sequence to transform into triplet patterns. A refinement move is triplet adaptation through first-notes omission, enabling mid-phrase access to the triplet version of a line.

# Chapter 21 — Half-time quarter-note triplet feel

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch21.md`._

Chapter 21 covers half-time quarter-note triplet feel through rhythmic practice centered on time-zone isolation. The method: isolate and master a single time zone using Tune 9 (Twinkle Twinkle), systematically advance through additional zones, then combine multiple zones by varying between them. Load-bearing concepts are time zones as rhythmic practice vehicles and composition as benchmark for mastery: students compose their own scale and intervallic sequences. This reflects the characteristic pedagogical move of building technique through self-generated material, where ability to compose variations demonstrates mastery of the underlying feel.

# Chapter 22 — Time zones (polyrhythms)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch22.md`._

Chapter 22 teaches time zones / polyrhythms through temporal displacement: constructing and feeling multiple time signatures layered against a steady four-beat foundation. Three primary construction methods are introduced: additive (building 9 notes by adding to eighth-note patterns), subtractive (removing notes to create 10-note or 12-note groupings), and displacement (offsetting phrases by eighth-note triplet intervals). The characteristic move is feel-first learning: polyrhythmic awareness develops through repetition and articulation variation in the foundational 5-against-4 exercise. Load-bearing terminology centers on polyrhythmic ratios (5, 9, 10, 12 notes over 4 beats) and construction vocabulary (additive, subtractive, offset).

# Chapter 23 — More time zones

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch23.md`._

Chapter 23 (More Time Zones) teaches polyrhythmic approaches to scale sequences by applying odd-grouping subdivisions (5, 7, 9) to scalar material, establishing frameworks like 5/2 four-note sequences and nine-note patterns whose beat span depends on tempo. Modes written in quintuplets extend polyrhythmic practice into fragmented forms: broken quintuplets, septuplets, nonuplets, each deployable across two, three, or four-beat metric contexts. Key terminology: quintuplets, septuplets, nonuplets, elastic time, and the concept of rhythmic ear training as distinct from melodic practice. The characteristic move is systematic exploration of multiple time zones, treating the same material across different metric containers. The chapter emphasizes that polyrhythmic mastery is a long-range pursuit, not a weekly drill.

# Chapter 24 — Broken quintuplets, septuplets, and nonuplets

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch24.md`._

Chapter 24 (Broken quintuplets, septuplets, nonuplets) teaches the practice and perception of irregular note groupings through a three-stage approach: any notes, then single chord, finally over tunes. Expansion and contraction are introduced as twin cognitive tools: taking a fixed group of notes and reinterpreting it across different beat spans, from one beat to four beats, or compressed into 1 1/2 beats, dissolves the strangeness of odd tuplets. The characteristic move is metric displacement, where exercises cross the 4/4 bar line by starting early or late, building polyrhythmic independence. Load-bearing terminology centers on expansion/contraction as a reframing strategy positioning irregular tuplets as flexible rescalings of familiar melodic shapes.

# Chapter 25 — Switching gears

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch25.md`._

Chapter 25 (Switching gears) introduces the distinction between downbeat and upbeat playing as separate technical exercises, establishing downbeats and upbeats as load-bearing terminology for rhythmic control. The chapter isolates each element first then progresses to mixing downbeats and upbeats on a single tune, integrating these isolated skills into fluid quarter-note soloing. This reflects a characteristic pedagogical move of building technique from component parts into cohesive musical phrases.

# Chapter 27 — Downbeat – upbeat (quarter-note playing)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch27.md`._

Chapter 27 (Downbeat-upbeat / quarter-note playing) teaches quarter-note playing and rhythmic economy as tools for musical expression and flexibility, emphasizing that less-is-more restraint allows breathing room and dialogue with the rhythm section. Core techniques: playing tunes feeling in four, two, or one beat per measure while tempo stays constant; collapsing the pulse at fast tempos to combat the eighth-note trap; using varied metronome placements (on 2 and 4, off-beats, or omitted entirely) to develop independence. Characteristic moves are pedagogical: using limitation as teacher (restricting to half notes and quarter notes; starting with accents and filling around them) and emphasizing relaxation as cognitive prerequisite to time mastery. Key terminology: feeling in one/two/four, in back of the time, on top of the time, eighth-note trap.

# Chapter 28 — Rhythmizing Scale Sequences

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch28.md`._

Chapter 28 (Rhythmizing scale sequences) teaches systematically varying a core pattern through modal transposition across scale degrees, note omission, rests, directional changes, and varied entry points to expand aural vocabulary. Characteristic moves center on using sus4 chords to isolate pure scale relationships without avoid-note constraints, and generating unexpected polyrhythmic groupings (groups of seven from strategic note omission). Key terminology: modal transposition, note omission, polyrhythmic approaches applied to sequences. Practice methodology emphasizes internalization first (familiarizing with the sequence), then deliberate exploration through 12 composed modal sequences played over backing tracks.

# Chapter 29 — Conceptual Practice and Rhythmic Grammar

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch29.md`._

Chapter 29 (Conceptual Practice and Rhythmic Grammar) establishes six core constraints on beat placement: never starting or ending on beat one, varying between predominantly eighth notes and passages without them, and manipulating chord anticipation (from half-beat to three-beat offsets) or late resolution (one to four beats). The chapter teaches that silence and endings are active compositional choices: rests frame the listener's attention, and knowing when to end a solo (leaving periods at the ends of musical sentences and paragraphs) invites the next idea rather than forcing continuation. Together these principles form a rhythmic vocabulary for storytelling on the instrument.

      "id": "jerry-bergonzi:melodic-rhythms-vol-4:rhythmic-grammar",
      "name": "Rhythmic Grammar and Phrasing Constraint System",
      "summary": "Six explicit phrase-placement constraints from Chapter 29 functioning as compositional grammar for jazz improvisation: beat-avoidance, density variation, anticipation offsets, late-resolution offsets, silence as choice, eighth-note trap avoidance.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "constraint-beat-one-avoidance",
          "name": "Beat-One Start/End Avoidance"
        },
        {
          "id": "constraint-density-variation",

        },
        {

          "name": "Chord Anticipation Offset"
        },
        {
          "id": "constraint-late-resolution",
          "name": "Late Resolution"
        },
        {
          "id": "constraint-silence-as-choice",
          "name": "Silence and Endings as Active Choices"
        },
        {
          "id": "constraint-eighth-note-trap-avoidance",
          "name": "Eighth-Note Trap Avoidance via Quarter-Note Playing"
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "beat-one-phrase-boundary-rule",
          "name": "Beat-One Phrase Boundary Constraint",
          "summary": "Never begin or end a phrase on beat one.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 29,
              "topic": "Beat placement rules",
              "quote_excerpt": "Never start on beat one. Never end on beat one",
              "page": 116
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "anticipation-grammar-rule",
          "name": "Chord Anticipation Grammar Rule",
          "summary": "Practice anticipating chord arrivals at offsets from 1/2 to 3 beats.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 29,
              "topic": "Beat placement rules",
              "quote_excerpt": "Anticipate chords by 1/2 beat, by 1 beat, by 1 1/2 beats, by 2 beats",
              "page": 116
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "late-resolution-grammar-rule",
          "name": "Late Resolution Grammar Rule",
          "summary": "Resolve to chord tones late by 1 to 4 beats.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 29,
              "topic": "Beat placement rules",
              "quote_excerpt": "Resolve late by 1 beat, 2 beats, 3 beats",
              "page": 116
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "density-variation-rule",
          "name": "Eighth-Note Density Alternation",
          "summary": "Vary between predominantly eighth-note passages and passages without eighth notes.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityCeiling"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 29,
              "topic": "Beat placement rules",
              "quote_excerpt": "Play predominantly eighth notes. Play without using eighth notes",
              "page": 116
            },

              "chapter_n": 27,
              "topic": "Less Notes / Breathing Room",
              "quote_excerpt": "Very often, less is more. Playing less notes at times allows the music to breath",
              "page": 110
            }
          ]
        },
